title: Characterization of errors in photon-heralded quantum operations between non-interacting quantum emitters
authors: Mahsa Karimi, Samuel Mister, Christoph Simon, Stephen C. Wein
abstract: We develop an analytic perturbative framework that enables the analysis of small Markovian errors in probabilistic, photon-heralded quantum operations between non-interacting emitters. Building on and extending the Zero-Photon-Generation (ZPG) framework, we derive closed-form perturbative solutions that capture both ideal (zero-order) and noisy (low-order) gate dynamics conditioned on time-integrated photon counting. Our framework provides analytic solutions to process matrices and Pauli error weights up to leading order, bridging the gap between detailed physical imperfections of a system and its corresponding abstract Pauli noise models. Moreover, our framework captures imperfections across the full physical system stack, from photon generation to optical manipulation. We benchmark the resulting perturbative predictions on a repeat-until-success CZ gate against numerical simulations, demonstrating accurate modeling of source-induced noise, and then apply the same framework to analyze coherent phase-shifter miscalibrations as a representative example of optical-manipulation errors. The methods developed in this work enable physics-informed parameter tuning to optimize gate designs and develop tailored quantum error correction protocols toward fault-tolerant quantum computing using hybrid light--matter quantum systems.
summary: This work introduces an analytic perturbative framework to quantify errors in quantum gates implemented using photon-heralded processes between quantum emitters. By extending the ZPG framework, the authors derive closed-form expressions for error weights, allowing for physics-informed optimization of gate designs for fault-tolerant quantum computing.
main_problem: The paper aims to characterize small Markovian errors in probabilistic, photon-heralded quantum operations between non-interacting quantum emitters.
main_result: The authors developed an analytic perturbative framework that successfully maps microscopic hardware imperfections (like source noise or phase miscalibrations) to abstract qubit-level noise models (Pauli error weights).
method: The core method involves developing an analytic perturbative expansion built upon the Zero-Photon-Generation (ZPG) framework to derive closed-form solutions for process matrices and error weights.
model_or_system: The system involves hybrid light-matter quantum systems using non-interacting emitters, single photons, and photon-number-resolving detectors. The dynamics are governed by a Lindblad master equation, analyzed via the ZPG framework.
key_observables: Process matrices ($\chi$ matrix), Pauli error weights ($\chi_{P,P}$), state fidelities ($F_{	ext{state}}^m$), and average gate fidelities ($F_{	ext{avg}}$).
important_parameters: Error strengths ($\gamma_k$), noise parameters ($\gamma_1, \gamma_2, \gamma_3$), and the low-noise regime ($\epsilon$ expansion).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on perturbative expansions, valid in the low-noise regime, and often assumes ideal detector performance initially.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the conceptual pipeline from physical models to fault-tolerant design, and show quantitative comparisons between analytical perturbative predictions and exact numerical simulations for various noise sources.
paper_structure: The paper builds conceptually from the general ZPG framework to derive perturbative solutions for conditional maps, applies these solutions to benchmark specific gates (like CZ), and then analyzes various physical error sources (spin relaxation, dephasing, phase shifts) to calculate resulting Pauli error weights.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous, analytical tool to bridge the gap between complex, physical hardware imperfections in quantum optics and the abstract mathematical tools required for quantum error correction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Conceptual pipeline from physical models to fault-tolerant quantum-computing design. Device-level descriptions are mapped to open-system quantum dynamics, which are then reduced to quantum-information metrics such as process matrices or stochastic Pauli error models. These qubit-level descriptions provide input for quantum-error-correction analysis and ultimately fault-tolerant logic. This work targets the highlighted step for probabilistic light–matter architectures by mapping hardware imperfections to leading-order analytic process matrices. (b) Relation to prior approaches. The Zero-Photon Generator (ZPG) framework gives analytic solutions under restricted error models [26],...

FIG. 2: Illustration of the general scenario captured by the ZPG framework [27]. The framework considers M emitters where each emitter can emit a photon into N ≥M different modes. The emitted photons interfere and are then measured with photon-number resolv- ing detectors (PNRDs) producing a pattern m ∈NN. The initial state ρinit of the ensemble of emitters collapses into a state propor- tional to Pm[ρinit]. The probability of obtaining measurement pat- tern m is tr(Pm[ρinit]).

FIG. 3: (a) A degenerate level structure of an emitter with spin- selective polarization emission for implementing the two-qubit RUS gate, together with the corresponding photonic integrate circuit im- plementing a dual-rail Bell-state measurement in the photonic X ba- sis. Here, U denotes the interferometer unitary, defined in Eq. (22). (b) A list the successful photon-detection patterns and the corre- sponding spin qubit operations produced by the RUS gate. Here S = diag(1, i) (and S†) is a required local Clifford correction. The operators Di are linear combinations of lowering operators associ- ated with different photon emitter transitions, where the coefficients are determined by the...

FIG. 4: Demonstration of the Pauli error weights associated with different sources of noise. Here γ1 denotes the excited-state spin relaxation noise strength defined in Eq. (25), γ2 and γ3 are the spin dephasing rate and emitter optical dephasing rate, respectively (see Eqs. (26) and (27)), and γ is the optical decay rate defined in Eq. (23). Panels (a)–(e) showcase the effect of each individual error, when the rest are set to zero. In panels (a)–(e), the analytical expression (represented by the solid line) corresponds to the solution that we obtain with our approach up to the fourth order. FIG. 5: The Pauli errors associated with the phase shifter error in the RUS gate. (a) The successful pattern (1010) has only a nonzero diagonal ZZ Pauli error weight, while the failure pattern (2000) re- mains unaffected by this imperfection. (b) Corresponding error table for the successful pattern.
